text: The video shows a fluorescent colorectal anastomosis during a high anterior resection for sigmoid cancer. The colon appears unhealthy, indicating the need for the anastomosis to be redone. Fluorescence angiography is used to visualize venous ischemia, highlighting the dynamic inflow patterns of fluorescence in the bowel segment.

The surgeon prepares the left mesocolon intercorporeally after high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, with visible good perfusion of the marginal artery. Indocyanine green (ICG) angiography is used to confirm the perfusion.

Using the Novadax endoscopic system, the surgeon performs perfusion angiography with near-infrared imaging to assess ICG inflow at the designated proximal transection site. The arterial inflow pattern indicates good perfusion, guiding the stapling for proximal transection within the well-perfused area. After specimen removal, the proximal colon is prepared with a purse string and anvil. During relaparoscopy, the anvil in the proximal descending colon is aligned with the circular stapler head inserted transanally into the rectum.

The surgeon switches to the near infrared view to repeat ICG angiography of the colorectal anastomosis. The colon surrounding the anvil shows delayed fluorescence compared to adjacent tissues, gradually brightening over thirty seconds. The stapler is fired, and the team examines the anastomosis for potential issues.

The surgeon examines the bowel, noting patchy fluorescence in the segment compared to the more proximal colon, indicating suboptimal arterial inflow. Closer inspection of the mesocolon reveals an area of encroachment at the same level, potentially caused during splenic flexure mobilization or specimen extraction. This injury likely preserved arterial inflow but compromised venous outflow.

The surgeon examines the bowel under white light, revealing signs of venous ischemia despite some arterial perfusion indicated by fluorescence. The colon appears unhealthy, with visible mucosal congestion suggesting potential anastomotic failure.

The video shows the affected segment of the colon where ICG has perfused into the tissue but is trapped due to venous congestion, indicating venous ischemia. The surrounding normal intestinal segments have cleared the ICG, highlighting the impaired outflow in the affected area. The surgeon decides to redo the anastomosis due to the compromised perfusion.

The surgeon performs a proximal transection across the descending colon to create a new colonic conduit. A further transection is made in the distant sigmoid for better access to the colorectal anastomosis. The mesorectum is dissected to ensure the distal transection is below the previous staple line. The rectal stump is freshened to implant the second anastomosis into healthy rectal tissue.

The surgeon redoes the anastomosis, with the spike of the circular stapler head emerging through the rectal stump. The proximal colonic segment, now from the very proximal descending colon near the splenic flexure, is positioned to reach the top of the rectal stump. A suture is placed in a small serosal tear in the splenic flexure area.

The surgeon performs a redo of the ICG angiogram, revealing a proper pattern of dynamic inflow along the colon. The colon exhibits fluorescence indicating improved perfusion. Postoperative outcomes are noted, including discharge on day three without a defunctioning stoma.
